author: Tertullian
book: On the Resurrection of the Flesh.

classification.cautionReason: No major mainstream heresy warning is attached by the preview classifier.

lineage: On the Resurrection of the Flesh.
Our Bodies, However Mutilated Before or After Death, Shall Recover Their Perfect Integrity in the Resurrection. Illustration of the Enfranchised Slave.

title: Our Bodies, However Mutilated Before or After Death, Shall Recover Their Perfect Integrity in the Resurrection. Illustration of the Enfranchised Slave.

source.title: ANF03. Latin Christianity: Its Founder, Tertullian

verses.book: On the Resurrection of the Flesh.
verses.text: We now come to the most usual cavil of unbelief.

verses.book: On the Resurrection of the Flesh.
verses.text: If, they say, it be actually the selfsame substance which is recalled to life with all its form, and lineaments, and quality, then why not with all its other characteristics?

verses.book: On the Resurrection of the Flesh.
verses.text: Then the blind, and the lame, and the palsied, and whoever else may have passed away with any conspicuous mark, will return again with the same.

verses.book: On the Resurrection of the Flesh.
verses.text: What now is the fact, although you in the greatness of your conceit thus disdain to accept from God so vast a grace?

verses.book: On the Resurrection of the Flesh.
verses.text: Does it not happen that, when you now admit the salvation of only the soul, you ascribe it to men at the cost of half their nature?

verses.book: On the Resurrection of the Flesh.
verses.text: What is the good of believing in the resurrection, unless your faith embraces the whole of it?

verses.book: On the Resurrection of the Flesh.
verses.text: If the flesh is to be repaired after its dissolution, much more will it be restored after some violent injury.

verses.book: On the Resurrection of the Flesh.
verses.text: Greater cases prescribe rules for lesser ones.

verses.book: On the Resurrection of the Flesh.
verses.text: Is not the amputation or the crushing of a limb the death of that limb?

verses.book: On the Resurrection of the Flesh.
verses.text: Now, if the death of the whole person is rescinded by its resurrection, what must we say of the death of a part of him?

verses.book: On the Resurrection of the Flesh.
verses.text: If we are changed for glory, how much more for integrity!

verses.book: On the Resurrection of the Flesh.
verses.text: Any loss sustained by our bodies is an accident to them, but their entirety is their natural property.

verses.book: On the Resurrection of the Flesh.
verses.text: In this condition we are born.

verses.book: On the Resurrection of the Flesh.
verses.text: Even if we become injured in the womb, this is loss suffered by what is already a human being.

verses.book: On the Resurrection of the Flesh.
verses.text: Natural condition is prior to injury.

verses.book: On the Resurrection of the Flesh.
verses.text: As life is bestowed by God, so is it restored by Him.

verses.book: On the Resurrection of the Flesh.
verses.text: As we are when we receive it, so are we when we recover it.

verses.book: On the Resurrection of the Flesh.
verses.text: To nature, not to injury, are we restored; to our state by birth, not to our condition by accident, do we rise again.

verses.book: On the Resurrection of the Flesh.
verses.text: If God raises not men entire, He raises not the dead.

verses.book: On the Resurrection of the Flesh.
verses.text: For what dead man is entire, although he dies entire?

verses.book: On the Resurrection of the Flesh.
verses.text: Who is without hurt, that is without life?

verses.book: On the Resurrection of the Flesh.
verses.text: What body is uninjured, when it is dead, when it is cold, when it is ghastly, when it is stiff, when it is a corpse?

verses.book: On the Resurrection of the Flesh.
verses.text: When is a man more infirm, than when he is entirely infirm?

verses.book: On the Resurrection of the Flesh.
verses.text: When more palsied, than when quite motionless?

verses.book: On the Resurrection of the Flesh.
verses.text: Thus, for a dead man to be raised again, amounts to nothing short of his being restored to his entire condition,—lest he, forsooth, be still dead in that part in which he has not risen again.

verses.book: On the Resurrection of the Flesh.
verses.text: God is quite able to re-make what He once made.

verses.book: On the Resurrection of the Flesh.
verses.text: This power and this unstinted grace of His He has already sufficiently guaranteed in Christ; and has displayed Himself to us (in Him) not only as the restorer of the flesh, but as the repairer of its breaches.

verses.book: On the Resurrection of the Flesh.
verses.text: And so the apostle says: “The dead shall be raised incorruptible” (or unimpaired).

verses.book: On the Resurrection of the Flesh.
verses.text: But how so, unless they become entire, who have wasted away either in the loss of their health, or in the long decrepitude of the grave?

verses.book: On the Resurrection of the Flesh.
verses.text: For when he propounds the two clauses, that “this corruptible must put on incorruption, and this mortal must put on immortality,” he does not repeat the same statement, but sets forth a distinction.

verses.book: On the Resurrection of the Flesh.
verses.text: For, by assigning immortality to the repeating of death, and incorruption to the repairing of the wasted body, he has fitted one to the raising and the other to the retrieval of the body.

verses.book: On the Resurrection of the Flesh.
verses.text: I suppose, moreover, that he promises to the Thessalonians the integrity of the whole substance of man.

verses.book: On the Resurrection of the Flesh.
verses.text: So that for the great future there need be no fear of blemished or defective bodies.

verses.book: On the Resurrection of the Flesh.
verses.text: Integrity, whether the result of preservation or restoration, will be able to lose nothing more, after the time that it has given back to it whatever it had lost.

verses.book: On the Resurrection of the Flesh.
verses.text: Now, when you contend that the flesh will still have to undergo the same sufferings, if the same flesh be said to have to rise again, you rashly set up nature against her Lord, and impiously contrast her law against His grace; as if it were not permitted the Lord God both to change nature, and to preserve her, without subjection to a law.

verses.book: On the Resurrection of the Flesh.
verses.text: How is it, then, that we read, “With men these things are impossible, but with God all things are possible;” and again, “God hath chosen the foolish things of the world to confound the wise?”

verses.book: On the Resurrection of the Flesh.
verses.text: Let me ask you, if you were to manumit your slave (seeing that the same flesh and soul will remain to him, which once were exposed to the whip, and the fetter, and the stripes), will it therefore be fit for him to undergo the same old sufferings?

verses.book: On the Resurrection of the Flesh.
verses.text: He is instead thereof honoured with the grace of the white robe, and the favour of the gold ring, and the name and tribe as well as table of his patron.

verses.book: On the Resurrection of the Flesh.
verses.text: Give, then, the same prerogative to God, by virtue of such a change, of reforming our condition, not our nature, by taking away from it all sufferings, and surrounding it with safeguards of protection.

verses.book: On the Resurrection of the Flesh.
verses.text: Thus our flesh shall remain even after the resurrection—so far indeed susceptible of suffering, as it is the flesh, and the same flesh too; but at the same time impassible, inasmuch as it has been liberated by the Lord for the very end and purpose of being no longer capable of enduring suffering.
